stem: A hospital uses a TSH blood test to screen for hypothyroidism. The test has a sensitivity of 93% and a specificity of 89%. The prevalence of hypothyroidism in the patient population is estimated to be between 2.8% and 5.1%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has hypothyroidism? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(hypothyroidism | positive test) ranges from 0.196 to 0.312 as prevalence varies from 2.8% to 5.1%. Point estimate at 4.0% prevalence: 0.261.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.11.
The confidence interval [0.196, 0.312] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (93%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
